gang-gang-cockatoo.size: Medium parrot; smaller than most cockatoos, compact build
gang-gang-cockatoo.habitat: Cool, wet forests and woodlands, particularly alpine bushland in south-eastern Australia
gang-gang-cockatoo.diet: Seeds and berries gathered from trees and shrubs
gang-gang-cockatoo.migration: Largely resident; may make local seasonal movements between altitudes

cockatiel.size: Small to medium parrot with a long tail
cockatiel.diet: Seeds and plant matter; a seed-eating parrot
cockatiel.migration: Nomadic across inland Australia, moving in response to food and water availability

budgerigar.size: Small, long-tailed parrot; one of the world's smallest parrots
budgerigar.habitat: Drier parts of inland Australia — arid and semi-arid scrublands and grasslands
budgerigar.migration: Nomadic, moving opportunistically across Australia in response to rainfall and food

australian-king-parrot.size: Large parrot endemic to eastern Australia
australian-king-parrot.habitat: Humid, heavily forested upland regions of eastern Australia, including eucalyptus and rainforest edges
australian-king-parrot.diet: Fruits and seeds gathered from trees or on the ground
australian-king-parrot.migration: Largely resident within its eastern Australian range

crimson-rosella.habitat: Mountain forests and gardens in eastern and south-eastern Australia; also introduced to New Zealand and Norfolk Island

eastern-rosella.habitat: Open woodlands, gardens, and farmland in south-eastern Australia; established in New Zealand

splendid-fairywren.habitat: Arid and semi-arid scrublands and dry forests across inland and western Australia

pied-currawong.size: Robust crow-like bird, averaging around 48 cm (19 in) long
pied-currawong.habitat: Forested areas of eastern Australia, from mature forest to urban parks and gardens
pied-currawong.diet: Omnivorous — berries, seeds, invertebrates, bird eggs, juvenile birds, and young marsupials
pied-currawong.migration: Generally sedentary; highland populations move to lower elevations in cooler months

australian-raven.habitat: Open woodlands, transitional zones, and urban areas including major Australian cities
australian-raven.diet: Omnivorous — fruits, seeds, lizards, chicks, small mammals, carrion, and human food waste
australian-raven.migration: Territorial and resident; does not migrate

willie-wagtail.habitat: Most open habitats across Australia, New Guinea, and nearby islands; common in lawns, parks, and gardens
willie-wagtail.diet: Insects; an insectivorous bird that hunts in open habitat

magpie-lark.size: Medium-sized passerine, similar in build to a small crow
magpie-lark.habitat: Widespread across Australia, Timor, and southern New Guinea; open country near water
magpie-lark.diet: Invertebrates and other small prey foraged on the ground

noisy-miner.habitat: Dry, open eucalypt forests without dense understory in eastern and south-eastern Australia; also suburban parks and gardens
noisy-miner.diet: Mainly nectar, fruit, and insects gleaned from eucalypt foliage

rainbow-bee-eater.size: Small, slender bee-eater with elongated central tail feathers
rainbow-bee-eater.habitat: Open country, woodland edges, and coastal areas across Australia
rainbow-bee-eater.diet: Bees, wasps, and other flying insects caught in the air
rainbow-bee-eater.migration: Migratory in southern Australia, moving north in winter; resident in the tropics

tawny-frogmouth.size: Large-headed, stocky bird; one of Australia's largest frogmouths
tawny-frogmouth.habitat: Forests, woodlands, and gardens across mainland Australia and Tasmania
tawny-frogmouth.diet: Insects, worms, and small vertebrates caught at night

powerful-owl.habitat: Primary forests with tall native trees in coastal and Great Dividing Range areas of south-eastern and eastern Australia
powerful-owl.diet: Primarily arboreal mammals, especially small to medium-sized marsupials; these make up about three-quarters of the diet
powerful-owl.migration: Resident; territorial with a large home range

white-bellied-sea-eagle.size: Up to 90 cm (35 in) long; wingspan up to 2.2 m (7.2 ft); weight up to 4.5 kg (9.9 lb)
white-bellied-sea-eagle.habitat: Coasts and major waterways from India and Sri Lanka through South-East Asia to Australia
white-bellied-sea-eagle.diet: Primarily fish (about half the diet), plus waterbirds, reptiles, carrion, and other animals

australian-pelican.size: Large waterbird; has the longest bill of any living bird
australian-pelican.habitat: Inland and coastal waters of Australia and New Guinea; also Fiji and parts of Indonesia
australian-pelican.diet: Mainly fish; also consumes birds and scavenges for scraps
australian-pelican.migration: Largely nomadic across Australia, moving in response to water availability

black-swan.size: Large waterbird with black plumage and a red bill
black-swan.habitat: Lakes, rivers, wetlands, and estuaries, mainly in south-east and south-west Australia
black-swan.migration: Nomadic within Australia, with erratic movements dependent on climatic conditions

maned-duck.habitat: Widespread across Australia in wetlands, grasslands, and open woodland near water
maned-duck.diet: Grasses, clover, and other plant matter; a grazing dabbling duck
maned-duck.migration: Largely resident; nomadic in response to drought and flooding

australian-wood-duck.habitat: Widespread across Australia in wetlands, grasslands, and open woodland near water
australian-wood-duck.diet: Grasses, clover, and other plant matter; a grazing dabbling duck
australian-wood-duck.migration: Largely resident; nomadic in response to drought and flooding

pacific-black-duck.habitat: Wetlands, lakes, rivers, and estuaries across Australia, New Zealand, New Guinea, and Pacific islands
pacific-black-duck.diet: Aquatic plants, seeds, insects, and small invertebrates

masked-lapwing.habitat: Open fields, grasslands, wetland margins, and urban parks across Australia, New Zealand, and New Guinea
masked-lapwing.diet: Insects, worms, and other invertebrates found on the ground

brolga.size: Tall crane with long legs, slender neck, and long beak
brolga.habitat: Wetlands, floodplains, grasslands, and open country in tropical and south-eastern Australia and New Guinea
brolga.diet: Omnivorous — plant matter, invertebrates, and small vertebrates
brolga.migration: Resident in the tropics; some seasonal movements in the south

australasian-gannet.habitat: Open waters above the continental shelf along southern and eastern Australia and around New Zealand; nests on coastal islands
australasian-gannet.diet: Mainly squid and forage fish caught by high-speed plunge diving
australasian-gannet.migration: Disperses widely at sea after breeding; young birds range broadly before returning to breed

little-penguin.size: Smallest penguin species in the world
little-penguin.habitat: Coastal waters and burrows on shorelines of southern Australia and New Zealand
little-penguin.diet: Small fish, squid, and krill caught by diving during the day
little-penguin.migration: Marine; returns to shore burrows each night; does not undertake long migrations

superb-lyrebird.size: One of the world's largest songbirds, with an elaborate tail
superb-lyrebird.diet: Insects, worms, and other invertebrates scratched from the forest floor

satin-bowerbird.habitat: Forests and woodland edges of eastern Australia
satin-bowerbird.diet: Fruits, berries, insects, and other invertebrates
satin-bowerbird.migration: Largely resident; some altitudinal movement in winter

regent-bowerbird.habitat: Rainforest and adjacent dense scrub in eastern Australia

crimson-chat.habitat: Arid and semi-arid shrublands and saltbush plains of inland Australia
crimson-chat.migration: Nomadic, moving in response to rainfall and food availability

new-holland-honeyeater.habitat: Heathlands, banksia scrub, and gardens across southern Australia
new-holland-honeyeater.diet: Nectar supplemented by insects and spiders

kea.habitat: Native forests across the three main islands of New Zealand
kea.diet: Omnivorous — fruit, seeds, nectar, insects, and occasionally carrion

tui.habitat: Various open and semi-open environments; cliffs and rock ledges used for roosting and breeding
tui.diet: Seeds and plant material; also scavenges opportunistically
tui.migration: Largely resident with some dispersal by feral populations

north-island-brown-kiwi.size: Medium-sized flightless bird; lays eggs among the largest relative to body size of any bird species
north-island-brown-kiwi.habitat: Forests and scrublands across the northern two-thirds of New Zealand's North Island
north-island-brown-kiwi.diet: Worms, insects, berries, and other invertebrates probed from the soil at night

black-browed-albatross.size: Large seabird; the most widespread and common albatross
black-browed-albatross.habitat: Open oceans of the Southern Hemisphere; breeds on subantarctic islands
black-browed-albatross.migration: Wide-ranging at sea; circumnavigates the Southern Ocean outside the breeding season

laysan-albatross.size: Large seabird; smaller than most albatrosses, with a gull-like appearance
laysan-albatross.habitat: North Pacific Ocean; breeds almost entirely on the Northwestern Hawaiian Islands
laysan-albatross.diet: Squid, fish, and fish eggs taken from the ocean surface
laysan-albatross.migration: Ranges widely across the North Pacific between breeding seasons

common-tern.habitat: Breeds on beaches, islands, and flat poorly vegetated ground near water across Europe, Asia, and North America; winters in coastal tropical regions
common-tern.diet: Mainly fish caught by plunge-diving; also molluscs, crustaceans, and invertebrates
common-tern.migration: Strongly migratory; breeds in temperate and subarctic regions, winters in coastal tropical and subtropical areas

sooty-tern.habitat: Tropical oceans worldwide; breeds on islands throughout the equatorial zone
sooty-tern.diet: Fish and other prey skimmed from the ocean surface
sooty-tern.migration: Largely pelagic; can fly for years without landing, only returning to land to breed

great-frigatebird.size: Large, lightly built seabird up to 105 cm long
great-frigatebird.habitat: Tropical Pacific and Indian Oceans; breeds on remote islands such as Hawaii, Galápagos, and Seychelles
great-frigatebird.diet: Primarily fish (especially flying fish) taken in flight from the ocean surface; occasionally kleptoparasitic
great-frigatebird.migration: Largely sedentary near breeding colonies; ranges within 80 km of colony or roost

magnificent-frigatebird.size: 89–114 cm (35–45 in) long; wingspan 2.17–2.44 m (7–8 ft); largest frigatebird species
magnificent-frigatebird.habitat: Tropical and subtropical waters off the Americas; also Galápagos and Cape Verde Islands
magnificent-frigatebird.diet: Fish (often flying fish) taken in flight from the ocean surface; also practices kleptoparasitism
magnificent-frigatebird.migration: Largely resident near tropical coasts; wide-ranging at sea but tied to breeding islands

red-footed-booby.habitat: Tropical oceans worldwide; breeds colonially on isolated coastal islands
red-footed-booby.diet: Flying fish and other fish caught by diving from above the ocean surface
red-footed-booby.migration: Resident near tropical breeding islands; ranges widely at sea

brown-booby.habitat: Inshore tropical and subtropical waters worldwide; nests on the ground on islands
brown-booby.diet: Small fish caught by plunge-diving, especially when prey is driven to the surface by predators
brown-booby.migration: Largely resident in tropical range; some post-breeding dispersal

king-penguin.size: Second largest penguin species, smaller than the Emperor Penguin
king-penguin.habitat: Subantarctic islands including South Georgia, southern Argentina, Macquarie Island, and the Falkland Islands
king-penguin.diet: Mainly lanternfish, squid, and krill; dives repeatedly to over 100 m, sometimes exceeding 300 m
king-penguin.migration: Resident on subantarctic breeding islands; forages at sea between colonies

gentoo-penguin.habitat: Subantarctic islands and the Antarctic Peninsula
gentoo-penguin.migration: Largely resident on breeding islands; forages in surrounding seas

chinstrap-penguin.habitat: Islands and shores in the Southern Pacific and Antarctic Oceans
chinstrap-penguin.migration: Largely resident near Antarctic and subantarctic breeding sites; disperses at sea in winter

adelie-penguin.habitat: Entire coast of Antarctica; one of the most southerly distributed penguins
adelie-penguin.migration: Breeds on the Antarctic coast; disperses northward onto sea ice in winter

rockhopper-penguin.habitat: Subantarctic and southern temperate islands in the Atlantic, Pacific, and Indian Oceans
rockhopper-penguin.diet: Krill, squid, fish, and other small marine prey
rockhopper-penguin.migration: Leaves breeding islands after the season; ranges widely at sea in winter

great-grey-owl.habitat: Boreal and montane forests across the Northern Hemisphere, in both Eastern and Western Hemispheres
great-grey-owl.diet: Small mammals, especially voles, caught by hunting over open areas
great-grey-owl.migration: Largely resident; some irruptive movements southward in years of low prey abundance

wild-turkey.size: Heaviest member of the order Galliformes; the heaviest wild game bird native to North America
wild-turkey.habitat: Forests, open woodlands, and forest edges across North America
wild-turkey.diet: Omnivorous — acorns, seeds, berries, insects, and small vertebrates

rook.habitat: Farmland, pasture, and open country across the Palearctic from Scandinavia and western Europe to eastern Siberia; nests in tall trees near farms or villages
rook.diet: Mainly grubs and soil-based invertebrates; also cereals and other plant material
rook.migration: Mainly resident; northernmost populations migrate south to avoid harsh winters
